speaker: interviewer
text: Jerry Goose. Tape number three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jerry, you were with 27 other people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in that small woods. How did you organize yourself? Who was the leader? How did you make decisions?

speaker: interviewee
text: What we're just together because it was no way to stay longer. The-- the-- the-- the farmers threat us. I mean, you have to eat. And we eat just what we got from the-- from the farm, potatoes. Not what the farmer give. We stole the potatoes. And the farmer was nice. They could see they're stealing potatoes, but they was nice to us. And later, start getting too cold, and we thought-- thought to do something. So then we are-- we are decided we going cross the-- the deep water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the river or the lake?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [? Happened, ?] but other time, I wouldn't remember. River [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a nice water, you know. And then we have to-- we decide to go about 2 o'clock in the morning where to everybody sleeps because the Ukrainian or the [? nationalists ?] or the-- or the-- where the Ukrainian, where they-- you know, where they working, where they want to be Ukrainian-- I don't know how to say in English. Wherever is. Then we have to-- we go in the night, in [INAUDIBLE], we went through the village, and the water we crossed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What village? Which village was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Which village?

speaker: interviewee
text: What beside-- the-- the village, I could not remember the village. I don't remember. Maybe I don't know then, too, because just the villages-- I never. Then we came at this other side water. And we-- we-- we already tired. We find a little bush and made a little fire to warm up yourself. And I [? won't excuse ?] for the things. There was a lot of lice. They took out their shirts with the pants and dried out pants because I got just one shirt with-- with one pair of pants because they made me run away from the home. I don't got too much. And the other people, too. And we got a little, a few pieces of potatoes, and we start eating. In the morning, we don't saw a shepherd drive the-- the cattle to the-- to the-- the grass. And we don't saw them because maybe he was tired and everything. And this was about 9, 10 o'clock. And they-- and we could hear shooting. Halt! Halt! Halt! Halt! And before we left the place, we-- we made a thing-- we-- we decided when something happen, when we was every time on the run, we should come in-- in one village is a woods. And there is a doctor with a family stays there. We find out this way. And everybody-- what-- wherever we're running, we should get-- to come together for the-- in this place. For this place, what we was, it about 25 miles to the other one. And [INAUDIBLE] was sitting through the day in the bush and then the night they go there. When the Germans start shooting on us to the day-- because the Germans came to the-- to the farmers to collecting bread for the army and some other things. That's the time they was in the village. In fact, when they-- when they go and tell a Jew-- when they see a Jew and they tell the Germans, the Germans give you a pound of sugar. Is anytime a Ukraine [? nationalist ?] see a Jew, then-- and tell the German, they give him a pound of sugar, and they-- they kill the Jew and everything. If he killing a Jew, he wouldn't-- they don't get them [INAUDIBLE]. [NON-ENGLISH] makes you one or the other one. When the 27-- we last-- we-- yes. I-- when they start shooting, I run one corner. The other one run in the other corner. We do not run for the other one. And I just naked the way I was. And I run into a farmer.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were naked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we dried out the thing by the fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: The clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I run into a farmer. They-- he start and he say-- say, what happen with you? I say, the Germans. Please give me something. Give me some-- a pair-- a pair of pants, or give me something. Well, these farmers, they poor, too. They are. They make-- clothing by yourself. Then they give me a pair of old pair of pants, one, and what they can. And they give me a shirt and a-- a shoes. They-- they got shoes. They make me shoes just from the-- from the woods. They made the shoes. They give me old kind tied together with a-- with a string. And I went to the place. To the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the doctor. It was maybe, then, about 11 o'clock in the night when I came. And I came down. I see the doctor's not there anymore. I was still-- his fire. What-- see, anybody in the woods warms with fire, with the wood, and they make hot, warm. Cooking, everything with wood. I saw I don't have to look for matches because I wouldn't have matches either. I don't have [INAUDIBLE]. Then I saw there is a few coals still there, and I made fire. I think about-- about 1 o'clock, I heard the leaves making noise. Then, I thought somebody coming. Before, I think maybe these people what went with me, maybe they come, coming still.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then they come, and I runned away from the fire. Nobody should see me because when you see the fire in the night, they could see me. And you know, who you [? think would come? ?] Come a-- a girl, maybe a six-year-old, and a boy, maybe from 11 years old to 10 years old. And they come over, and I come over to them closer. And I say, what you like, kids? How come you're here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody tell me the doctor-- doctor here because I-- the girl said, I-- I fell. And my knee blood is swollen like a-- like a balloon, and I want the doctor could-- should help me. I said, dear kids. I say, the doctor is gone. He's gone to make for winter a place in the ground, some other places. And you sleep here over with-- with me together. Then the-- the usual, the way you come to visit, and everybody go pick together a little-- a little bit wood to make fire. The kids want to go and get some wood. I say, kids, you don't need the wood. I got enough fire. And they going to be warm. I thought they going to be warm. And this was a frosty night were there. And I going to remember all my time. I was crying and they was crying. Here the-- the heat was give you from the wood, and the back was freezing. But I got just-- you know, he give me a shirt with a pair of pants. And the girl got a little dress and [INAUDIBLE]. Well, we hardly could wait to the morning. And I went with the-- with the girl to the village. And there the village was a Polish village. Polish people not against Jew, at least on Ukraine because they afraid for the Ukraine and thought they was kill them, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went to the whole village. You know how many potatoes we begged up? Begged. You know, beg, begged to give us. Maybe three potatoes. And we came to the village, back to the woods, and-- and I got some, a little pot of water. I don't remember what. We warm up, make some warm water with soup. And then later, they went to look for the doctor to the day with the woods, not to the village because other way the day-- in the day, they would, could get killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And between time, about two, three days, from the 27, we got about 16. They caught-- the caught the-- the Jews. They tie them together by the legs for them and carried them around. But tie them together to the truck, and brought them to the village. Until they come to the village, they were dead. That's what they say, one story again.

speaker: interviewer
text: They dragged them behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: They dragged them. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you met up with a partisan group.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Then with the-- they start-- the Germans start looking for us in the woods. And I thought there's no way to stay. Was everybody from the-- who they came, they went. They say, we going to look for another places, and they say, they going to look for other places. And I left alone, and now I find out is--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the group of 16 broke up.

speaker: interviewee
text: They broked up. And I-- I thought, and somebody tell me is a partisan, a group underground. And well, you could go in. You'd be lucky. This, they tell me, the Polish people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and they tell me what-- what they staying. They staying in this kind woods, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What woods? What kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: That what-- what I was. They-- they call them [NON-ENGLISH] Woods, you know the Ukraine woods, whatever they call them.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, they there and tried to go over and back. And maybe they let you, they accept you. Well, and I went there. And I-- and they don't let me through. There was some-- before I went to them, they don't stay always. The partisan, they watch them, you know, all around. They ask me what-- what I want. I say, I've been a butcher for trade, in my trade. I could help get meat for the partisan and make some meat. Well, with-- like that. OK. They brought me over to the commander, to the sergeant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. See I don't remember anything because I-- I don't remember these days, too. It's another one day [? there for then. ?] You know what I mean? So already it was, what-- 40 year, 50 years, or 60.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, he-- he brought us over there, and they asked me-- what you know about-- about meat? I say I know take out the skin and cut them and do everything. They accept me. Was this a Jewish partisan group?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Ukrainian partisan group. Were there-- were you the only Jewish person there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was about three or four, five of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: The two know that. Not the all. Because we was in different groups. I was in the group by the cattle. Other-- other ones used to go and fight. Between them and the other ways, everybody in some-- they-- they was-- when they was walking, they walking for five miles. So many horses and so many partisans. Maybe was 2,000 partisans. And when we was walking, the-- the Germans know-- thought we 20 people, partisans walking. And we was just about maybe 1,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you all men or were there women or children there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was women, too. No children. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the group, the partisan group you were with? Who was the leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kovpak. That's [? the cell. ?] That's the main leader, the-- the general. Then like a-- then we went from city to city, and just they got on the Germans. Took away-- take away from them everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me some of the operations that you were involved in.

speaker: interviewee
text: What the-- the-- the partisans?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: What the partisans doing-- see, Moscow, the Russian, just give you orders. They come in. When you needed something, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this guns, we have to find by yourself in the Germans. But medicine, they just parachute us. And we went in from one city, we find out was Germans to the city and what-- we could go in not too many, not a big fight to put it in. Then we walked in there, and went to the-- took away the guns from them, took away the cattle from them, took away everything, put in our wagons, and we went farther. Yes, there was bridges. We just put them-- under bombs. The-- the trains-- we put on trains, bombs in them. Blow them up. That's with we used to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your job? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I looked after just the-- the food, the meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get the meat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I explained you, any city we walked in, any Germans, they have-- used to have a-- what they call them-- a-- a place where they got some cattle, you know, horses and-- and food in what the-- they support them on. Then we ring-- we ring when we're taking the city in a-- a-- like you say, what another ring? What you-- how you say that?

words.text: What you-- how you say that?

speaker: interviewer
text: A circle.

speaker: interviewee
text: A circle. Then some Germans, they run away before, for they know the partisans coming. Is not too many there. And some, they do a little shoot up, and then they walking out of there. And then we're going in, and they-- they call it [POLISH], they called them in-- in Polish. We go in. We take away the meat, whatever thing we needed, buggies, horse and buggies, with everything. We going farther. In city to city. We-- anything-- they-- in Moscow, they give us the orders to what train should come with the Germans, what that. And we just put them in-- bombs between them. And that-- that's the-- that's the order what we really got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get weapons from-- did the Russians supply you with weapons, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We have to took from the Germans, taking weapons.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- when you killed the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing medicine. Just like they-- they running away. I was in Fanny's city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same what I-- the-- Fanny-- Fanny was looked after the Russian, what they got the-- them and everything there in a Russian place. What I say, Russian? German. She was working for them, Fanny. In fact, Kovpak was-- he spoke to her. He want to know what was-- was hiding, what this, and that. I think-- I don't know if she tell him some things or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we went city to city and orders what they have to be-- trains to take, you know, blow out, and other-- and bridges. And we went so far to the Carpathian they call them, were the hills.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Carpathian Mountains.

speaker: interviewee
text: Carpathian. Yes. And then, the Germans got us in, and they don't let us out from that. They start bombing us into anything. Then they give us our order from Moscow. And everybody should go wherever you could. Jews go up in this place. And then, we was like a-- we walk around as sheeps with no-- you know, don't know what to turn. And I was lucky because I-- the partisan what I was working with then, they know I know butcher. And they say-- they come over to me, and say, listen. You know-- you know-- the butcher. You know how to be a butcher, you come with us. And-- and we going to make it to go out from the-- from the-- for the hills. You know, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I say, we-- the first thing, we just prayed God to find warm place with a farmer. We just got one street straight from the hill to a farmer. Then we went to the farmer, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was about eight-- seven, eight. I don't remember correctly. [NON-ENGLISH] went, and we got-- and looked. I was the one what I went because I was dressed in German, dressed like Germans, the clothing I-- what they killed the Germans, I put on the clothing, the pants, the shoes. And I went and looked in the window, and no Germans there, no young people, Ukraine. Then I knocked on the windows, and opened the door, the-- the farmer. And I tell him, listen, I am not a German. I am not-- I am just a partisan, and I want you-- we are seven, eight people-- you should get me from your place-- what is 20 miles another bush-- to bring us over in the-- and took. And I don't want to see your brother, Ukraine brother, the [NON-ENGLISH]. And you get us to the [NON-ENGLISH], to your brothers, so we get to the German, you're going to get the-- the first, the first, you're going to be shot. And then your whole house is going to be fire. Well, that's what we went from village to village. During one place, I come to the same-- do the same job in a village. I got-- the first farmer was at the end from the village. I knocked the door. And I always dreamed when I could meet some Jewish people. I was feet up to walk already, go around. And happened, I looked in the window, and I say-- and I think at myself, is a Jew there. I was so excited. And I knocked the door, and the farmer opened-- opened the door. He was scared. He was pale and everything. I said, listen. We don't kill people. We don't do nothing. Just I want you to show us how to go. He showed the-- the place to go from this woods to the other one. And like-- I tell him the whole story. And then I tell him, tell me who's this guy what was run away next-- in the other room. I say, I think he's a Jew. And I've been a Jew too, and I want to spoke to him. When this guy heard I've been a Jew, he runned out from the room and talked to me. And I tell him, we are seven, eight people, and we have some guns, and we have that. Well, he said, oh my, friend, I was waiting for people like you all that time. We got in this woods-- [NON-ENGLISH] was the name. This I remember now. There's this five, six families there, lawyers and doctors and kids, small kids and big, about 30 people. And they will be happy to see you. Well, then I say, OK. I don't bother anymore with the farmer. I say, you're OK. Show us where you live. That was maybe about 20 miles from the village, is a little woods, woods were there. And he brought me over, and I see the [INAUDIBLE]. They saw like-- I don't know what to do with them. Maybe you like it. Maybe you like this. Maybe you like that-- to the other farmer, the partisan. [INAUDIBLE] we was not doing [INAUDIBLE]. We got with us food. In fact, we give them food a little bit. And they start begging us to stay with them. Then the-- the Ukrainian people say-- start-- well, they begging us and they warn us. They warn me with guns. Then I left there. And I got the gun. And they say--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know went back with the partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. You stayed with the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I stay with the people. And they say, you know what, Jerry? We going. We are [NON-ENGLISH], Ukrainian. Nobody going to touch us. Our brothers what they killing Jewish, they wouldn't kill us. And we going farther. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was with them, and they were-- and we were-- and I-- and I brought them food, how much they want. What we used to do, just so the part is done, I used to do for the Germans. We used to go three, four people. I used to have a gun. Another, they got a gun, and some got sticks, like a gun in the night. And we came to the village where the German have the food and the cattle and everything, their place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we went-- we went and we [? mask. ?] We came to the village, is the-- this guy what looks after the cattle, and I-- and I ask him, what's the guy what have the store with the food, with everything. He tell us, three miles from this place, he has a house and live there. Then we went to the-- to this-- to this guy what he has the-- the store with the all food. And we give him a order. You should open up this store. And we took how much we want food. And we take all of us in [INAUDIBLE]. And the farmer, [INAUDIBLE] in this place what the German got the-- [INAUDIBLE], the horses with the cattle, and put them on the buggy. And putting everything on the buggy, the food, anything, and brought over the families. And they got enough to eat for awhile.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let stop.
